content:
I've been burnt by running make rustavailable and then being unable to find "Rust support" under "General setup" many times, until I realized that the march support wasn't enabled yet. This is in the context of experimenting on arm64 and riscv64 SBCs. Yes, I now realize that it needs to be enabled per-arch, but didn't know about it earlier and thought that it was already done.

I doubt CONFIG_RUST=y will be in the defconfig for a few years. Until then, maybe we should let the curious minds know _why_ Rust is _available_ but can't be enabled.

What I propose for this:

Require a .config file for make rustavailable to work. Though this check can be in the beginning, I think it's best if it is done _after_ all checks complete without errors. There, we can check the .config file for anything that is interfering with CONFIG_RUST=y.

I am creating this thread (forgot what Zulip calls it) to get a concensus on what maintainers want and will draft my RFC on the GitHub issue tracker appropriately. So, what are your thoughts?

While I also often have the problem that the RUST option is not available, I use make menuconfig to then search (just press /) for RUST there. It then shows this:

  │ Symbol: RUST [=y]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     │
  │ Type  : bool                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          │
  │ Defined at init/Kconfig:1884                                                                                                                                                                                                                          │
  │   Prompt: Rust support                                                                                                                                                                                                                                │
  │   Depends on: HAVE_RUST [=y] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && !MODVERSIONS [=n] && !GCC_PLUGINS [=n] && !RANDSTRUCT [=n] && (!DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n] || PAHOLE_HAS_LANG_EXCLUDE [=n])                                                                        │
  │   Location:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           │
  │     -> General setup                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  │
  │ (1)   -> Rust support (RUST [=y])                                                                                                                                                                                                                     │
  │ Selects: CONSTRUCTORS [=y]

There you can exactly see what prevents it from being enabled/what you need to do in order for it to be enabled.

Are you aware of this feature?
I also think that adding a small check to the rustavailable target that scans for a .config file and checks if rust is also configured would be a good idea.
But I would like to preserve the current usage of rustavailable to ensure that people have the correct versions of tools.

One thing I really hate is that the kernel compiles when I have the RUST option set, but I forget to run "make LLVM=1 rustavailable". Especially with the new approach of constantly updating the minimum required rustc version.

Yes, I'm aware that it shows up in make *config but I missed that when I used ./scripts/config --enable RUST in the package's spec file for Rocky Linux. I kept seeing that Rust is available but the .config file then installed in /boot would not have CONFIG_RUST at all, not even "is not set".

I would've loved a clearer solution to this :)

Benno Lossin said:

But I would like to preserve the current usage of rustavailable to ensure that people have the correct versions of tools.

Maybe we can add another script and call it from the makefile :)

There, we can check the .config file for anything that is interfering with CONFIG_RUST=y.

If I understand correctly, that is not really related to Rust. As Benno points out, the / functionality is quite good for that.

I would've loved a clearer solution to this :)

What I would like is that RUST=y means the build will fail if Rust is not available. However, we were requested to do it otherwise. But perhaps at some point in the future we can change it to work like that.

Ariel Miculas said:

One thing I really hate is that the kernel compiles when I have the RUST option set, but I forget to run "make LLVM=1 rustavailable". Especially with the new approach of constantly updating the minimum required rustc version.

It is not required to run rustavailable. Perhaps you mean that RUST gets disabled if Rust is not available? That is something that, indeed, I would like that it worked differently, but please see my previous reply.

Miguel Ojeda said:

There, we can check the .config file for anything that is interfering with CONFIG_RUST=y.

If I understand correctly, that is not really related to Rust. As Benno points out, the / functionality is quite good for that.

From an outsider's POV, if make rustavailable exits cleanly, I expect the modified config to work (after doing a make LLVM=1 olddefconfig).

Miguel Ojeda said:

It is not required to run rustavailable. Perhaps you mean that RUST gets disabled if Rust is not available? That is something that, indeed, I would like that it worked differently, but please see my previous reply.

Yes, that's what I mean, I checkout a different branch, forget to check if make rustavailable runs ok, compile the kernel, and it doesn't have rust support (it got disabled it the meantime because I forgot to upgrade/downgrade the rustc compiler).
I understand it was a requirement to do it this way, I still hate it though :)

Being more specific, since I work with non-interactive environments, I expect the modification I made with the scripts/config script to work. If then CONFIG_RUST=y cannot be retained by olddefconfig (in context of compiling natively on ARM64 due to the support being WIP and unmerged), I think it is the responsibility of the rustavailable target to notify the user that "your machine arch is ARM64 and that won't work at the moment". Yes, Rust is available but it feels rather pedantic to say "It's available but you can't use it."

You mean that rustavailable implies RUST can be enabled (i.e. take into account the rest of the Kconfig), but "Rust is available" means the toolchain only:

  rustavailable   - Checks whether the Rust toolchain is
                    available and, if not, explains why.

I agree it can be confusing and we could have added "toolchain" at the end or the middle, but then it would have been a bit too long (e.g. make rusttoolchainavailable).

Would it help if we at least add it to the message that gets printed when it exits cleanly? I think that could clarify things, i.e. something like:

$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
The Rust toolchain is available!

Miguel Ojeda said:

Would it help if we at least add it to the message that gets printed when it exits cleanly? I think that could clarify things, i.e. something like:

$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
The Rust toolchain is available!

Yes, that would be awesome! Maybe also clarify that the toolchain being enabled doesn't mean you can compile with Rust support though.

Something like this:

$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
The Rust toolchain is available!
This doesn't mean that you can compile with Rust support enabled. Please check the Kconfig for why, or why not.

Sounds good. Perhaps a bit shorter and mentioning the symbols helps:

$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
The Rust toolchain is available! (`CONFIG_RUST_IS_AVAILABLE=y`)
This does not imply Rust support (`CONFIG_RUST`) is enabled.

@Miguel Ojeda Understanding your stance on this, does this mean that the recommended method of checking why CONFIG_RUST can be enabled or not enabled is from make *config? Or should there be a separate make target for this too?

Though yes, checking with make *config is _one_ way, it is not used in build scripts for packages for obvious reasons. Instead the scripts/config script is used to toggle a config option. There, I still need a way to check if me appending CONFIG_RUST=y to .config will be respected or not.

Also,

Miguel Ojeda said:

Sounds good. Perhaps a bit shorter and mentioning the symbols helps:

$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
The Rust toolchain is available! (`CONFIG_RUST_IS_AVAILABLE=y`)
This does not imply Rust support (`CONFIG_RUST`) is enabled.

Should I send a PR for this?

Miguel Ojeda said:

I would've loved a clearer solution to this :)

What I would like is that RUST=y means the build will fail if Rust is not available. However, we were requested to do it otherwise. But perhaps at some point in the future we can change it to work like that.

I think what's being requested is allyesconfig enables Rust if Rust is available? Is it possible to achieve that while avoiding have oldconfig from removing CONFIG_RUSTwhen Rust toolchain is not available?

Or should there be a separate make target for this too?

Why should this be different than other Kconfig symbols? i.e. if I understand correctly, what you are asking for is a better/different way to check dependencies and so on, and that could be nice to add somewhere, but is not related to Rust, i.e. if it is added it should work for all symbols.

it is not used in build scripts for packages for obvious reasons

I am not sure I follow -- what exactly do you need to do/check in your build scripts?

There, I still need a way to check if me appending CONFIG_RUST=y to .config will be respected or not.

Note that scripts/config --enable or doing it manually does not guarantee it is respected for any symbol.

If you only need to assert that it is enabled "for real", you could sync the config and then check the .config.

Pratham Patel said:

Should I send a PR for this?

If you mean a patch, of course! Please read https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html and ideally add a Suggested-by: and Link: to Zulip (for instance, to my message above).

Or should there be a separate make target for this too?

Why should this be different than other Kconfig symbols? i.e. if I understand correctly, what you are asking for is a better/different way to check dependencies and so on, and that could be nice to add somewhere, but is not related to Rust, i.e. if it is added it should work for all symbols.

Yes, makes sense.

There, I still need a way to check if me appending CONFIG_RUST=y to .config will be respected or not.

Note that scripts/config --enable or doing it manually does not guarantee it is respected for any symbol.

Yes, I do agree with that. But the "hey why isn't this enabled when Rust is available?" _came_ from the misunderstanding that CONFIG_RUST_IS_ENABLED == CONFIG_RUST which wasn't obvious to me.

If you only need to assert that it is enabled "for real", you could sync the config and then check the .config.

Hmm... Maybe we can do this for all symbols in old*config targets? Could be a nice quality of life improvement.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Pratham Patel said:

Should I send a PR for this?

If you mean a patch, of course! Please read https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html and ideally add a Suggested-by: and Link: to Zulip (for instance, to my message above).

Sorry yes, this _will_ be my first patch haha. That brings up two questions I have:

  1. What tree should I base my patch off? Rust-for-Linux/linux or torvalds/linux? This question stems from the confusion that I don't see any explicit dependency on the Rust-for-Linux tree for my patch but also am aware that patches should be based off a maintainer's tree.
  2. If I should base against Rust-for-Linux/linux, should I send the patch in form of a PR? Or should I still email it to the rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org mailing list?
See https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing and https://rust-for-linux.com/branches
This is super confusing, even for people who use the system all the time. If CONFIG_RUST=y and rust is not available, we should throw an error rather than setting CONFIG_RUST=n silently.

Andreas Hindborg said:

This is super confusing, even for people who use the system all the time. If CONFIG_RUST=y and rust is not available, we should throw an error rather than setting CONFIG_RUST=n silently.

It's not even CONFIG_RUST=n, that entire line just gets removed. Not even a # CONFIG_RUST is not set :(
The later I understand but still a pain to deal with because of pedantics.
